content.text: Based on the information gathered, I can now explain
MCP exists to solve a fundamental problem:
AI models need access to real-world data and tools, but traditional integrations were fragmented, complex, and not designed for models

- Every AI application needed custom code to connect with each data source, API, and service
- If you had 10 AI apps and 100 data sources, you needed 1,000 separate integrations (10 × 100)
- Each integration was different, creating massive maintenance overhead
2. APIs Were Built for Developers, Not Models

to perform actions (technical implementation)
- But they don't tell models
- Traditional API routing breaks when AI is involved (e.g., "if weather in input" doesn't scale)

- There were many ways to connect clients to external services, but no standard approach
- Every framework had its own custom integration method
MCP addresses this challenge by providing a universal, open standard for connecting AI systems with data sources, replacing fragmented integrations with a single protocol
Custom integration for each AI app + data source pair
Write one MCP server, works everywhere
MCP wraps APIs into tool definitions models can reason about
Universal protocol for all MCP-compatible clients
Define tools once, let MCP handle the interface

- One MCP client connects to any compliant server
- Developers build tools once as an MCP server, any MCP-compatible platform can use them

- MCPs give AI applications the external context/capabilities they need to complete their mission
- Originally built to give LLMs like Claude access to more data sources
- Now evolved into a standard way for AI agents to access tools and context
3. Structures Access, Not Just Enables It

- MCP doesn't just give access—it structures it
- Standardizes context exchange between AI assistants and software environments
- Provides structured tools with clear inputs/outputs that models can understand

tell the system how to perform an action
-

allows the model to decide when that action is needed
- This missing decision layer is why MCP exists
Why It Matters: The USB-C Analogy
Just as USB-C provides a standardized way to connect electronic devices (monitors, chargers, keyboards)
MCP provides a standardized way to connect AI applications to external systems
Instead of building a new bridge every time, any AI that speaks "MCP" can connect to any tool that speaks "MCP"
MCP's aim is to help frontier models produce better, more relevant responses
by giving them access to the systems where data lives.

This enables AI assistants to:
- Access

in tools like GitHub, Slack, browsers
- Execute

by chaining multiple tools
- Work with
MCP is not about making agents easier or doing fancy things with LLMs. It's about making systems reusable, scalable, and interoperable
: Stack tools and data sources together like Lego bricks
: Not proprietary—community-driven with no vendor lock-in
: Standardized approach eliminates the effort of building custom integrations
: Once running, it's surprisingly smooth and reliable
AI models need to interact with the real world, but the existing infrastructure (APIs) wasn't designed for them
. MCP sits above APIs as a translation layer that converts them into structured tools models can understand and use reliably.
